title: Day 15 Vocabulary — Would Like To (100+ Words)

words.simpleMeaning: to politely ask for something

sentences.daily: I would like to request a day off.
sentences.office: She would like to request an extension on the deadline.
sentences.interview: I would like to request a moment to think.
sentences.business: We would like to request a formal quote.
sentences.formal: I would like to request your kind consideration.
sentences.informal: Can you request a table for two?

words.usageNote: 'Request' is more formal than 'ask'. Use in emails and official communication.

words.simpleMeaning: to organize or plan something

sentences.daily: I would like to arrange a family dinner.
sentences.office: She would like to arrange a team meeting.
sentences.interview: I would like to arrange a second interview.
sentences.business: We would like to arrange a product demo.
sentences.formal: I would like to arrange a formal introduction.
sentences.informal: Can you arrange a get-together?

words.usageNote: 'Arrange for' means to make preparations so that something happens.

words.simpleMeaning: to plan something for a specific time

sentences.daily: I would like to schedule a doctor's appointment.
sentences.office: She would like to schedule a performance review.
sentences.interview: I would like to schedule an interview at your convenience.
sentences.business: We would like to schedule a site visit.
sentences.formal: I would like to schedule a board meeting.
sentences.informal: Let's schedule a coffee chat.

words.usageNote: American English: 'schedule' /ˈskedʒuːl/. British: /ˈʃedjuːl/.

words.simpleMeaning: to talk about something in detail

sentences.daily: I would like to discuss my weekend plans.
sentences.office: She would like to discuss the project budget.
sentences.interview: I would like to discuss my experience.
sentences.business: We would like to discuss a partnership.
sentences.formal: I would like to discuss the terms of the agreement.
sentences.informal: Would you like to discuss it over lunch?

words.usageNote: 'Discuss' is never followed by 'about': discuss the issue (NOT discuss about).

words.simpleMeaning: to formally request a job or opportunity

sentences.daily: I would like to apply for a library membership.
sentences.office: She would like to apply for a promotion.
sentences.interview: I would like to apply for the position of team leader.
sentences.business: We would like to apply for a business loan.
sentences.formal: I would like to apply for the grant.
sentences.informal: Should I apply for that course?

words.usageNote: 'Apply for' = apply for a job/position. 'Apply to' = apply to a place/organization.

words.simpleMeaning: to ask someone to come somewhere

sentences.daily: I would like to invite you to my birthday party.
sentences.office: She would like to invite the client to the office.
sentences.interview: I would like to invite you to a second round.
sentences.business: We would like to invite you to our annual event.
sentences.formal: I would like to invite you to address the conference.
sentences.informal: Would you like to invite him?

words.usageNote: 'Invite someone to something' — always use 'to' after invite.

words.simpleMeaning: to present something for someone to accept

sentences.daily: I would like to offer you some tea.
sentences.office: She would like to offer a solution to the problem.
sentences.interview: I would like to offer my skills to your company.
sentences.business: We would like to offer you a special discount.
sentences.formal: I would like to offer my sincere apologies.
sentences.informal: Would you like to offer help?

words.usageNote: 'Offer to do' = volunteer to do something. 'Offer someone something' = present something to someone.

words.simpleMeaning: to put forward an idea for consideration

sentences.daily: I would like to suggest a restaurant for dinner.
sentences.office: She would like to suggest a new approach.
sentences.interview: I would like to suggest ways to improve efficiency.
sentences.business: We would like to suggest a revised timeline.
sentences.formal: I would like to suggest that we reconsider this.
sentences.informal: Would you like to suggest something?

words.usageNote: 'Suggest + gerund' OR 'suggest + that clause': suggest going / suggest that we go.

words.simpleMeaning: to state that something is definitely true or going to happen

sentences.daily: I would like to confirm my booking.
sentences.office: She would like to confirm the meeting time.
sentences.interview: I would like to confirm the interview details.
sentences.business: We would like to confirm our order.
sentences.formal: I would like to confirm receipt of your email.
sentences.informal: Can you confirm the plan?

words.usageNote: Common in professional emails: 'I would like to confirm...' is very standard.

words.simpleMeaning: to decide not to do something already planned

sentences.daily: I would not like to cancel our plans.
sentences.office: She would like to cancel the afternoon meeting.
sentences.interview: I would like to cancel and reschedule our meeting.
sentences.business: We would not like to cancel the contract.
sentences.formal: I would like to formally cancel my subscription.
sentences.informal: Do you want to cancel?

words.usageNote: British English: 'cancelled'. American English: 'canceled'. Both are correct.

words.simpleMeaning: to give someone information about something

sentences.daily: I would like to inform you about the party.
sentences.office: She would like to inform the team of the changes.
sentences.interview: I would like to inform you of my availability.
sentences.business: We would like to inform you of a price change.
sentences.formal: I would like to inform you that your application was successful.
sentences.informal: Would you like to inform everyone?

words.usageNote: Very formal expression. Often used in official letters/emails.

words.simpleMeaning: to ask for food/goods to be brought or made

sentences.daily: I would like to order a pizza.
sentences.office: She would like to order new office supplies.
sentences.interview: I would like to order my thoughts before answering.
sentences.business: We would like to order 500 units.
sentences.formal: I would like to place an order for your services.
sentences.informal: Would you like to order something?

words.usageNote: 'Order' in a restaurant = ask for food. 'Place an order' = formal business language.

words.simpleMeaning: to book something in advance

sentences.daily: I would like to reserve a table for four.
sentences.office: She would like to reserve the conference room.
sentences.interview: I would like to reserve some time to ask questions.
sentences.business: We would like to reserve a spot at the exhibition.
sentences.formal: I would like to reserve your services for next month.
sentences.informal: Can you reserve a seat for me?

words.usageNote: 'Reserve' is slightly more formal than 'book'. Both mean the same.

words.simpleMeaning: to examine or investigate something

sentences.daily: I would like to explore new hobbies.
sentences.office: She would like to explore new marketing strategies.
sentences.interview: I would like to explore opportunities in this company.
sentences.business: We would like to explore a collaboration.
sentences.formal: I would like to explore all available options.
sentences.informal: Would you like to explore the city?

words.usageNote: 'Explore' is often used figuratively in professional settings: explore options/opportunities.

words.simpleMeaning: to give or add something to a shared effort

sentences.daily: I would like to contribute to this community project.
sentences.office: She would like to contribute ideas in the meeting.
sentences.interview: I would like to contribute my technical skills.
sentences.business: We would like to contribute to your growth.
sentences.formal: I would like to contribute to this academic discussion.
sentences.informal: Would you like to contribute something?

words.usageNote: 'Contribute to' = add to. Great word for interviews: 'I would like to contribute to...'

words.simpleMeaning: to make something better

sentences.daily: I would like to improve my communication skills.
sentences.office: She would like to improve team productivity.
sentences.interview: I would like to improve my performance consistently.
sentences.business: We would like to improve our customer satisfaction.
sentences.formal: I would like to improve the quality of our output.
sentences.informal: Would you like to improve your English?

words.usageNote: Very useful in interview context: 'I would like to improve...' shows ambition.

words.simpleMeaning: to make something clearer or easier to understand

sentences.daily: I would like to clarify my position.
sentences.office: She would like to clarify the instructions.
sentences.interview: I would like to clarify one point about my experience.
sentences.business: We would like to clarify the terms of the deal.
sentences.formal: I would like to clarify my earlier statement.
sentences.informal: Can you clarify what you mean?

words.usageNote: Use in meetings/interviews: 'I would like to clarify...' shows professionalism.

words.simpleMeaning: to say sorry for something

sentences.daily: I would like to apologize for being late.
sentences.office: She would like to apologize for the miscommunication.
sentences.interview: I would like to apologize for any confusion.
sentences.business: We would like to apologize for the delay.
sentences.formal: I would like to sincerely apologize for this error.
sentences.informal: Would you like to apologize?

words.usageNote: British spelling: 'apologise'. Both forms are accepted internationally.

words.simpleMeaning: to praise someone for an achievement

sentences.daily: I would like to congratulate my sister.
sentences.office: She would like to congratulate the team.
sentences.interview: I would like to congratulate you on your success.
sentences.business: We would like to congratulate our partners.
sentences.formal: I would like to congratulate you on this achievement.
sentences.informal: Would you like to congratulate him?

words.usageNote: 'Congratulate on' — always use 'on': congratulate on an achievement.

words.simpleMeaning: to present someone or something for the first time

sentences.daily: I would like to introduce my friend Priya.
sentences.office: She would like to introduce the new team member.
sentences.interview: I would like to introduce myself briefly.
sentences.business: We would like to introduce our new product line.
sentences.formal: I would like to formally introduce our new CEO.
sentences.informal: Would you like to introduce yourself?

words.usageNote: 'Introduce someone to someone' — always use 'to'.

words.simpleMeaning: to be present at an event or meeting

sentences.daily: I would like to attend the cultural festival.
sentences.office: She would like to attend the leadership workshop.
sentences.interview: I would like to attend the second interview.
sentences.business: We would like to attend the trade fair.
sentences.formal: I would like to attend the annual general meeting.
sentences.informal: Would you like to attend the party?

words.usageNote: 'Attend' is more formal than 'go to'. Use in professional contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to show or give something formally

sentences.daily: I would like to present my idea to the group.
sentences.office: She would like to present the quarterly report.
sentences.interview: I would like to present my portfolio.
sentences.business: We would like to present our proposal.
sentences.formal: I would like to present my findings.
sentences.informal: Would you like to present first?

words.usageNote: 'Present' as verb = /prɪˈzent/ (different stress from noun: /ˈprezənt/).

words.simpleMeaning: to suggest something as good or suitable

sentences.daily: I would like to recommend this restaurant.
sentences.office: She would like to recommend a new tool.
sentences.interview: I would like to recommend myself for this role.
sentences.business: We would like to recommend our premium service.
sentences.formal: I would like to recommend this candidate.
sentences.informal: Would you like to recommend a book?

words.usageNote: 'Recommend + gerund' OR 'recommend + that clause': recommend trying / recommend that you try.

words.simpleMeaning: to become a part of a group or activity

sentences.daily: I would like to join the yoga class.
sentences.office: She would like to join the project team.
sentences.interview: I would like to join your organization.
sentences.business: We would like to join the industry association.
sentences.formal: I would like to join the board of directors.
sentences.informal: Would you like to join us?

words.usageNote: 'Join' for groups/organizations. 'Join in' = participate in an activity already happening.

words.simpleMeaning: to make or become larger

sentences.daily: I would like to expand my knowledge.
sentences.office: She would like to expand the department.
sentences.interview: I would like to expand my skill set.
sentences.business: We would like to expand to new markets.
sentences.formal: I would like to expand on this point.
sentences.informal: Would you like to expand your horizons?

words.usageNote: 'Expand on' = explain in more detail. Very useful in meetings.

words.simpleMeaning: to discuss something to reach an agreement

sentences.daily: I would like to negotiate the price.
sentences.office: She would like to negotiate the contract.
sentences.interview: I would like to negotiate the salary package.
sentences.business: We would like to negotiate the terms.
sentences.formal: I would like to negotiate a mutually beneficial agreement.
sentences.informal: Would you like to negotiate?

words.usageNote: 'Negotiate with someone about something' — a key business skill.

words.simpleMeaning: to go to see a person or place

sentences.daily: I would like to visit my grandparents.
sentences.office: She would like to visit the client's office.
sentences.interview: I would like to visit your headquarters.
sentences.business: We would like to visit your factory.
sentences.formal: I would like to visit the site before making a decision.
sentences.informal: Would you like to visit the museum?

words.usageNote: 'Visit someone' = go to see someone. 'Pay a visit' = make a formal visit.

words.simpleMeaning: to go from one place to another

sentences.daily: I would like to travel to Manali.
sentences.office: She would like to travel for work.
sentences.interview: I would like to travel internationally for this role.
sentences.business: We would like to travel to meet our clients.
sentences.formal: I would like to travel as part of my duties.
sentences.informal: Would you like to travel abroad?

words.usageNote: British: 'travelled/travelling'. American: 'traveled/traveling'. Both correct.

words.simpleMeaning: to remain in a place or continue in a condition

sentences.daily: I would like to stay at home today.
sentences.office: She would like to stay late to finish the project.
sentences.interview: I would like to stay with this company long-term.
sentences.business: We would like to stay competitive.
sentences.formal: I would like to stay in touch with your team.
sentences.informal: Would you like to stay for dinner?

words.usageNote: 'Stay in touch' = keep in contact. Very useful phrase.

words.simpleMeaning: to come into contact with someone for the first time or by arrangement

sentences.daily: I would like to meet your family.
sentences.office: She would like to meet the new clients.
sentences.interview: I would like to meet the team I'll be working with.
sentences.business: We would like to meet to discuss the proposal.
sentences.formal: I would like to meet at your earliest convenience.
sentences.informal: Would you like to meet for coffee?

words.usageNote: 'Meet with' = have a formal meeting (American English). 'Meet' = encounter or get together.

words.simpleMeaning: having good manners; showing respect for others

sentences.daily: Be polite when talking to elders.
sentences.office: It is polite to say thank you after a meeting.
sentences.interview: I would like to be polite and professional.
sentences.business: Polite communication builds trust.
sentences.formal: A polite tone is expected in formal emails.
sentences.informal: He's always so polite.

words.usageNote: 'Polite' is an adjective. Adverb: 'politely'.

words.simpleMeaning: following official rules and customs; serious in manner

sentences.daily: I need a formal dress for the event.
sentences.office: Use formal language in official emails.
sentences.interview: This is a formal interview process.
sentences.business: A formal contract is required.
sentences.formal: I would like to make a formal complaint.
sentences.informal: Keep it formal, not casual.

words.usageNote: 'Formal' = following rules. 'Informal' = relaxed/casual. Know the context.

words.simpleMeaning: relating to a job or career; skilled and competent

sentences.daily: Act professional in all situations.
sentences.office: She has a very professional approach.
sentences.interview: I would like to maintain a professional attitude.
sentences.business: Professional behavior is essential.
sentences.formal: I would like to seek your professional advice.
sentences.informal: He looks so professional!

words.usageNote: Used as both adjective and noun: 'He is a professional.' / 'professional behavior'.

words.simpleMeaning: suitable and easy to use; not causing difficulty

sentences.daily: Is tomorrow convenient for you?
sentences.office: Let's find a convenient meeting time.
sentences.interview: I would like to schedule at a convenient time.
sentences.business: The location is convenient for both parties.
sentences.formal: I would like to meet at a time convenient for you.
sentences.informal: Is it convenient?

words.usageNote: 'At your convenience' = whenever is easiest for you. Common in formal emails.

words.simpleMeaning: to recognize the value or quality of something; to be grateful

sentences.daily: I would like to appreciate your help.
sentences.office: She would like to appreciate the team's effort.
sentences.interview: I appreciate the opportunity to be here.
sentences.business: We would like to appreciate your partnership.
sentences.formal: I would like to appreciate your kind gesture.
sentences.informal: Do you appreciate what he did?

words.usageNote: 'I appreciate it' = I am grateful. Very common and natural in formal English.

words.simpleMeaning: to like one thing better than another

sentences.daily: I would prefer to wake up early.
sentences.office: She would prefer to work from home.
sentences.interview: I would prefer a role with more responsibility.
sentences.business: We would prefer a shorter timeline.
sentences.formal: I would prefer to address this in writing.
sentences.informal: Would you prefer tea or coffee?

words.usageNote: 'Prefer + to + infinitive' OR 'prefer + gerund'. Both: prefer to go / prefer going.

words.simpleMeaning: to reply or react to something

sentences.daily: I would like to respond to your message.
sentences.office: She would like to respond to the complaint.
sentences.interview: I would like to respond to your question with an example.
sentences.business: We would like to respond to your proposal.
sentences.formal: I would like to respond to your inquiry promptly.
sentences.informal: Would you like to respond?

words.usageNote: 'Respond to' = reply to. More formal than 'reply'.

words.simpleMeaning: to politely refuse an offer or invitation

sentences.daily: I would like to decline this invitation politely.
sentences.office: She would like to decline the offer.
sentences.interview: I would like to decline and ask for more time.
sentences.business: We would like to decline the current terms.
sentences.formal: I would like to respectfully decline.
sentences.informal: Would you like to decline?

words.usageNote: 'Decline' is more polite and formal than 'refuse'.

words.simpleMeaning: to agree to receive or take something

sentences.daily: I would like to accept your invitation.
sentences.office: She would like to accept the new responsibilities.
sentences.interview: I would like to accept this job offer.
sentences.business: We would like to accept the terms.
sentences.formal: I would like to formally accept your proposal.
sentences.informal: Would you like to accept?

words.usageNote: 'Accept' + noun/gerund. Don't confuse with 'except' (= excluding).

words.simpleMeaning: to help someone do something

sentences.daily: I would like to assist you with this.
sentences.office: She would like to assist in the onboarding.
sentences.interview: I would like to assist the team wherever needed.
sentences.business: We would like to assist with the implementation.
sentences.formal: I would like to assist in resolving this matter.
sentences.informal: Would you like to assist?

words.usageNote: 'Assist' is more formal than 'help'. Use in professional communication.

words.simpleMeaning: to successfully complete or attain something

sentences.daily: I would like to achieve my fitness goals.
sentences.office: She would like to achieve the quarterly targets.
sentences.interview: I would like to achieve excellence in this role.
sentences.business: We would like to achieve market leadership.
sentences.formal: I would like to achieve this milestone.
sentences.informal: What would you like to achieve?

words.usageNote: 'Achieve a goal/target/result'. Common in interview: 'I would like to achieve...'

words.simpleMeaning: to have or live through something; knowledge gained from practice

sentences.daily: I would like to experience different cultures.
sentences.office: She would like to experience new challenges.
sentences.interview: I would like to bring my experience to your team.
sentences.business: We would like to experience your product firsthand.
sentences.formal: I would like to experience your services.
sentences.informal: Would you like to experience this?

words.usageNote: Noun AND verb. As verb: experience something = go through it. Very useful in interviews.

words.simpleMeaning: to grow or cause something to grow; to build a skill

sentences.daily: I would like to develop a new hobby.
sentences.office: She would like to develop her leadership skills.
sentences.interview: I would like to develop professionally in this role.
sentences.business: We would like to develop a new product.
sentences.formal: I would like to develop a comprehensive strategy.
sentences.informal: Would you like to develop your talents?

words.usageNote: 'Develop' suggests gradual progress. 'Develop a skill/product/strategy'.

words.simpleMeaning: to briefly refer to or state something

sentences.daily: I would like to mention one more thing.
sentences.office: She would like to mention the new policy.
sentences.interview: I would like to mention my achievements.
sentences.business: We would like to mention our success rate.
sentences.formal: I would like to mention that this is time-sensitive.
sentences.informal: Would you like to mention anything?

words.usageNote: 'Mention' = briefly note. 'Not to mention' = used for emphasis: It's expensive, not to mention risky.

words.simpleMeaning: to make something longer or bigger; to offer something

sentences.daily: I would like to extend the lease.
sentences.office: She would like to extend the project deadline.
sentences.interview: I would like to extend my gratitude.
sentences.business: We would like to extend our contract.
sentences.formal: I would like to extend a warm welcome.
sentences.informal: Would you like to extend the meeting?

words.usageNote: 'Extend a welcome/invitation/offer' = formal expressions. 'Extend a deadline' = postpone.

words.simpleMeaning: to choose from a group

sentences.daily: I would like to select a birthday gift.
sentences.office: She would like to select the best candidate.
sentences.interview: I would like to be selected for this role.
sentences.business: We would like to select a reliable vendor.
sentences.formal: I would like to select the most appropriate option.
sentences.informal: Would you like to select one?

words.usageNote: 'Select' = more formal than 'choose'. Common in formal/professional writing.

words.simpleMeaning: to take part in an activity or event

sentences.daily: I would like to participate in the quiz.
sentences.office: She would like to participate in the training.
sentences.interview: I would like to actively participate in team activities.
sentences.business: We would like to participate in the tender.
sentences.formal: I would like to participate in the conference.
sentences.informal: Would you like to participate?

words.usageNote: 'Participate in' — always followed by 'in'. More formal than 'take part in'.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling or showing thanks for something done or received

sentences.daily: I would like to express how grateful I am.
sentences.office: She is grateful for the opportunity.
sentences.interview: I am grateful for this interview opportunity.
sentences.business: We are grateful for your continued support.
sentences.formal: I would like to say how grateful I am for your assistance.
sentences.informal: I'm so grateful, thank you!

words.usageNote: 'Grateful for something' / 'grateful to someone'. Common in formal expressions of thanks.

words.simpleMeaning: a time when it is possible to do something

sentences.daily: I would like to take this opportunity.
sentences.office: She would like to use every opportunity to learn.
sentences.interview: I would like to thank you for this opportunity.
sentences.business: We would like to create new opportunities.
sentences.formal: I would like to take this opportunity to thank everyone.
sentences.informal: Don't miss this opportunity!

words.usageNote: 'Take/seize/grab an opportunity'. Often used in speeches: 'I'd like to take this opportunity to...'

words.simpleMeaning: information given in response to a product or performance

sentences.daily: I would like to give you my feedback.
sentences.office: She would like to receive feedback on her work.
sentences.interview: I would like to ask for feedback after the interview.
sentences.business: We would like to incorporate your feedback.
sentences.formal: I would like to formally provide my feedback.
sentences.informal: Can I give you some feedback?

words.usageNote: 'Feedback' is uncountable — don't say 'a feedback'. Say 'some feedback' or 'feedback'.

words.simpleMeaning: a formal meeting to assess a candidate for a job

sentences.daily: I have an interview tomorrow.
sentences.office: She would like to interview the candidates.
sentences.interview: I would like to do well in this interview.
sentences.business: We would like to schedule an interview.
sentences.formal: I would like to invite you for an interview.
sentences.informal: How was your interview?

words.usageNote: As verb: 'interview someone' = ask them questions to assess. As noun: 'attend/have an interview'.

words.simpleMeaning: fixed regular payment for employment

sentences.daily: I would like to increase my savings from my salary.
sentences.office: She would like to discuss her salary.
sentences.interview: I would like to negotiate the salary package.
sentences.business: We would like to offer a competitive salary.
sentences.formal: I would like to inquire about the salary range.
sentences.informal: What's the salary for this job?

words.usageNote: 'Salary' = monthly payment. 'Wages' = daily/weekly. 'Stipend' = training allowance.

words.simpleMeaning: an advancement to a higher position at work

sentences.daily: I would like to celebrate her promotion.
sentences.office: She would like to discuss her promotion.
sentences.interview: I would like to work towards a promotion.
sentences.business: We would like to announce a promotion.
sentences.formal: I would like to formally recommend him for a promotion.
sentences.informal: Did you get the promotion?

words.usageNote: 'Promotion' also means advertising: 'sales promotion'. Context determines meaning.

words.simpleMeaning: to help or encourage someone

sentences.daily: I would like to support my friend's dream.
sentences.office: She would like to support her team.
sentences.interview: I would like to support company goals.
sentences.business: We would like to support our clients.
sentences.formal: I would like to offer my full support.
sentences.informal: Would you like to support this cause?

words.usageNote: 'Support' can be noun or verb. 'I need your support' (noun). 'I will support you' (verb).

words.simpleMeaning: to finish doing something

sentences.daily: I would like to complete this course.
sentences.office: She would like to complete the report by Friday.
sentences.interview: I would like to complete assignments efficiently.
sentences.business: We would like to complete the project on time.
sentences.formal: I would like to complete this formality.
sentences.informal: Would you like to complete this?

words.usageNote: 'Complete' suggests thoroughness — more formal than 'finish'.

words.simpleMeaning: a planned piece of work with a specific goal

sentences.daily: I would like to work on a creative project.
sentences.office: She would like to lead the new project.
sentences.interview: I would like to share about a project I managed.
sentences.business: We would like to take on this project.
sentences.formal: I would like to present the project proposal.
sentences.informal: How's your project going?

words.usageNote: 'Project' as verb: /prəˈdʒekt/ = to estimate or display on screen. Different stress as noun.

words.simpleMeaning: the latest time or date for completing something

sentences.daily: I would like to finish before the deadline.
sentences.office: She would like to push the deadline slightly.
sentences.interview: I would like to always meet deadlines.
sentences.business: We would like to stick to the agreed deadline.
sentences.formal: I would like to request an extension on the deadline.
sentences.informal: What's the deadline for this?

words.usageNote: 'Meet a deadline' = finish on time. 'Miss a deadline' = fail to finish on time.

words.simpleMeaning: a plan or suggestion, especially in business

sentences.daily: I would like to make a proposal.
sentences.office: She would like to review the proposal.
sentences.interview: I would like to share a business proposal.
sentences.business: We would like to present our proposal.
sentences.formal: I would like to submit a formal proposal.
sentences.informal: What do you think of my proposal?

words.usageNote: 'Make/put forward/submit a proposal'. 'Proposal' also used for marriage: a marriage proposal.

words.simpleMeaning: an arrangement or contract between parties

sentences.daily: I would like to reach an agreement.
sentences.office: She would like to review the agreement.
sentences.interview: I would like to work in a team where there is agreement.
sentences.business: We would like to finalize the agreement.
sentences.formal: I would like to sign the agreement today.
sentences.informal: Are we in agreement?

words.usageNote: 'Reach/sign/finalize an agreement'. 'In agreement' = in accord with someone.

words.simpleMeaning: a way of doing or thinking about something

sentences.daily: I would like to take a new approach.
sentences.office: She would like to change her approach to the problem.
sentences.interview: I would like to explain my approach to team management.
sentences.business: We would like to suggest a different approach.
sentences.formal: I would like to present a fresh approach.
sentences.informal: What's your approach?

words.usageNote: Both noun and verb. 'A hands-on approach' = practical method.

words.simpleMeaning: a plan of when things will happen

sentences.daily: I would like to follow a daily schedule.
sentences.office: She would like to see the project schedule.
sentences.interview: I would like to work within the given schedule.
sentences.business: We would like to share our production schedule.
sentences.formal: I would like to review the revised schedule.
sentences.informal: What's your schedule today?

words.usageNote: Same word as verb (id:3). As noun: 'follow a schedule' / 'ahead of schedule'.

words.simpleMeaning: an advantage or profit gained from something

sentences.daily: I would like to understand the benefits.
sentences.office: She would like to explain the employee benefits.
sentences.interview: I would like to benefit the company with my skills.
sentences.business: We would like to benefit from this collaboration.
sentences.formal: I would like to highlight the key benefits.
sentences.informal: What are the benefits?

words.usageNote: 'Benefit from' = gain an advantage. Employee benefits = salary, insurance, leave.

words.simpleMeaning: a situation that makes it possible to do something

sentences.daily: I would like to grab this opportunity.
sentences.office: She would like to create new opportunities.
sentences.interview: I would like to use this opportunity well.
sentences.business: We would like to identify new opportunities.
sentences.formal: I would like to thank you for this opportunity.
sentences.informal: It's a great opportunity!

words.usageNote: 'Take/seize/grab an opportunity'. Already listed at id:49 but included here as it's a key word for this topic.

words.simpleMeaning: ready or eager to do something

sentences.daily: I am willing to learn new things.
sentences.office: She is willing to take extra responsibilities.
sentences.interview: I am willing to relocate if required.
sentences.business: We are willing to negotiate.
sentences.formal: I would like to express that I am fully willing.
sentences.informal: Are you willing to help?

words.usageNote: 'Willing to + infinitive'. 'Willingness' = noun form. Key word in job applications.

words.simpleMeaning: able to change or adapt easily

sentences.daily: I would like to have a flexible routine.
sentences.office: She would like to have flexible working hours.
sentences.interview: I am flexible about working hours.
sentences.business: We would like to offer a flexible payment plan.
sentences.formal: I would like to suggest a more flexible approach.
sentences.informal: Are you flexible this weekend?

words.usageNote: 'Flexible hours/schedule' = adjustable timing. Great word for job interviews.

words.simpleMeaning: doing something well without wasting time or effort

sentences.daily: I would like to be more efficient in my work.
sentences.office: She would like to make the process more efficient.
sentences.interview: I am an efficient and organized worker.
sentences.business: We would like to ensure efficient operations.
sentences.formal: I would like to propose a more efficient system.
sentences.informal: She's so efficient!

words.usageNote: 'Efficient' = doing things with minimum waste. 'Effective' = achieving results. Similar but different.

words.simpleMeaning: to formally suggest a plan or course of action

sentences.daily: I would like to propose a new idea.
sentences.office: She would like to propose a change in strategy.
sentences.interview: I would like to propose solutions to problems.
sentences.business: We would like to propose a joint venture.
sentences.formal: I would like to formally propose this motion.
sentences.informal: Would you like to propose a toast?

words.usageNote: 'Propose a toast' = raise a glass. 'Propose to someone' = offer marriage.

words.simpleMeaning: to find a solution to a problem

sentences.daily: I would like to resolve this misunderstanding.
sentences.office: She would like to resolve the conflict.
sentences.interview: I would like to help resolve team issues.
sentences.business: We would like to resolve this matter quickly.
sentences.formal: I would like to resolve this at the earliest.
sentences.informal: Would you like to resolve this?

words.usageNote: 'Resolve an issue/problem/conflict'. 'Resolution' = noun form.

words.simpleMeaning: to work jointly with others

sentences.daily: I would like to collaborate on a music project.
sentences.office: She would like to collaborate with other teams.
sentences.interview: I would like to collaborate and learn from others.
sentences.business: We would like to collaborate with your company.
sentences.formal: I would like to explore a collaborative arrangement.
sentences.informal: Would you like to collaborate?

words.usageNote: 'Collaborate on something / with someone'. 'Collaboration' = noun. Very valued in workplaces.

words.simpleMeaning: to give a task or responsibility to someone else

sentences.daily: I would like to delegate some tasks.
sentences.office: She would like to delegate responsibilities.
sentences.interview: I know how to delegate effectively.
sentences.business: We would like to delegate this to a specialist.
sentences.formal: I would like to formally delegate this authority.
sentences.informal: Would you like to delegate this to someone?

words.usageNote: 'Delegate a task to someone'. Key management skill.

words.simpleMeaning: to organize or control something

sentences.daily: I would like to manage my time better.
sentences.office: She would like to manage the new team.
sentences.interview: I would like to manage a project independently.
sentences.business: We would like to manage operations smoothly.
sentences.formal: I would like to manage this initiative.
sentences.informal: Can you manage this?

words.usageNote: 'Manage to do' = succeed in doing. 'Manage' also means to cope.

words.simpleMeaning: to give someone essential information; short

sentences.daily: I would like to give you a brief update.
sentences.office: She would like to brief the team.
sentences.interview: I would like to give a brief introduction.
sentences.business: We would like to provide a brief overview.
sentences.formal: I would like to brief the committee.
sentences.informal: Can you give me a brief summary?

words.usageNote: 'Brief someone' = give key info. 'In brief' = in summary.

words.simpleMeaning: to dedicate yourself to something; to promise

sentences.daily: I would like to commit to a healthier lifestyle.
sentences.office: She would like to commit to the project.
sentences.interview: I would like to commit fully to this role.
sentences.business: We would like to commit to quality service.
sentences.formal: I would like to commit to this partnership.
sentences.informal: Would you like to commit?

words.usageNote: 'Commit to + gerund/noun'. 'Commitment' = noun. Very powerful in interviews.

words.simpleMeaning: to officially join a course or program

sentences.daily: I would like to enroll in an English class.
sentences.office: She would like to enroll in a management course.
sentences.interview: I enrolled in a certification program.
sentences.business: We would like to enroll our staff.
sentences.formal: I would like to enroll in this program.
sentences.informal: Did you enroll yet?

words.usageNote: British: 'enrol'. American: 'enroll'. 'Enroll in' — always use 'in'.

words.simpleMeaning: in a polite and respectful manner

sentences.daily: Please speak politely.
sentences.office: She always communicates politely.
sentences.interview: Answer every question politely.
sentences.business: Always respond politely to clients.
sentences.formal: I would like to politely decline.
sentences.informal: Just ask politely!

words.usageNote: Adverb form of 'polite'. Remember to use politely in all formal communication.

words.simpleMeaning: to pause before doing something because of uncertainty

sentences.daily: I would not like to hesitate when speaking English.
sentences.office: Please do not hesitate to contact me.
sentences.interview: I would not like to hesitate in giving my answers.
sentences.business: Do not hesitate to reach out.
sentences.formal: Please do not hesitate to ask if you need anything.
sentences.informal: Don't hesitate — just go for it!

words.usageNote: 'Do not hesitate to...' = please feel free to. Common in formal emails.

words.simpleMeaning: in a courteous manner

sentences.daily: Ask politely for what you need.
sentences.office: Communicate politely with all colleagues.
sentences.interview: Respond politely to all questions.
sentences.business: Handle complaints politely.
sentences.formal: I would like to politely request your attention.
sentences.informal: Can you ask politely please?

words.usageNote: Adverb of polite. Use widely in professional contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: making someone feel at home; friendly and warm

sentences.daily: I would like to create a welcoming environment.
sentences.office: She has a very welcoming attitude.
sentences.interview: I would like to be part of a welcoming team.
sentences.business: We would like to create a welcoming space.
sentences.formal: I would like to extend a welcoming gesture.
sentences.informal: The people here are very welcoming!

words.usageNote: 'Welcoming atmosphere/environment'. Also 'welcome' as verb: 'We welcome you.'

words.simpleMeaning: suitable for a particular situation

sentences.daily: I would like to wear appropriate clothing.
sentences.office: Please use appropriate language.
sentences.interview: I would like to give appropriate answers.
sentences.business: We would like to take appropriate action.
sentences.formal: I would like to take the appropriate measures.
sentences.informal: Is this appropriate?

words.usageNote: 'Appropriate for / to'. Opposite: 'inappropriate'. Key word in formal communication.

words.simpleMeaning: an official and structured way of asking for something

sentences.daily: I would like to make a formal request.
sentences.office: She submitted a formal request for leave.
sentences.interview: I would like to formally request more information.
sentences.business: We would like to put in a formal request.
sentences.formal: I would like to submit a formal request in writing.
sentences.informal: Make a formal request if needed.

words.usageNote: 'Submit/make/put in a formal request'. Used when informal channels aren't enough.

words.simpleMeaning: the act of making something clearer or easier to understand

sentences.daily: I would like to seek clarification.
sentences.office: She would like to get clarification on the policy.
sentences.interview: I would like to seek clarification on this question.
sentences.business: We would like to request clarification on the terms.
sentences.formal: I would like to seek your clarification on this matter.
sentences.informal: Can you give me some clarification?

words.usageNote: 'Seek/request/ask for clarification'. Noun of 'clarify'.

words.simpleMeaning: help or support given to someone

sentences.daily: I would like to ask for your assistance.
sentences.office: She would like to provide assistance.
sentences.interview: I would like to offer assistance where possible.
sentences.business: We would like to request your assistance.
sentences.formal: I would like to request your kind assistance.
sentences.informal: Can I get some assistance here?

words.usageNote: 'Ask for/provide/need assistance'. Formal version of 'help'.

words.simpleMeaning: authorization to do something

sentences.daily: I would like to ask for your permission.
sentences.office: She would like to get permission to leave early.
sentences.interview: I would like to ask permission to ask a question.
sentences.business: We would like to seek permission to use the trademark.
sentences.formal: I would like to request your permission formally.
sentences.informal: Do I have your permission?

words.usageNote: 'Ask for/get/grant permission'. 'With your permission...' = formal phrase before doing something.

words.simpleMeaning: advice or direction given to someone

sentences.daily: I would like to seek your guidance.
sentences.office: She would like to provide guidance to the team.
sentences.interview: I would like to receive guidance from senior colleagues.
sentences.business: We would like to seek expert guidance.
sentences.formal: I would like to request your guidance on this issue.
sentences.informal: Can I get some guidance from you?

words.usageNote: 'Seek/offer/provide guidance'. Great word for professional growth conversations.

words.simpleMeaning: refined and professional in manner

sentences.daily: I would like to have a more polished appearance.
sentences.office: She gives polished presentations.
sentences.interview: I would like to come across as polished.
sentences.business: We would like to maintain a polished image.
sentences.formal: I would like to deliver a polished speech.
sentences.informal: You look so polished today!

words.usageNote: 'Polished presentation/speech/appearance'. Implies effort and refinement.

words.simpleMeaning: to make someone feel admiration or respect

sentences.daily: I would like to impress my parents.
sentences.office: She would like to impress the new client.
sentences.interview: I would like to impress the interviewer.
sentences.business: We would like to impress our investors.
sentences.formal: I would like to impress upon you the urgency.
sentences.informal: I want to impress him!

words.usageNote: 'Impress someone with something'. 'Impressed by/with' — both are correct.

words.simpleMeaning: to put money or effort into something for future benefit

sentences.daily: I would like to invest in my education.
sentences.office: She would like to invest in new technology.
sentences.interview: I would like to invest my skills in this company.
sentences.business: We would like to invest in your startup.
sentences.formal: I would like to invest in this initiative.
sentences.informal: Would you like to invest in this?

words.usageNote: 'Invest in something/someone'. 'Investment' = noun. Key business/finance word.

words.simpleMeaning: to start or introduce something new

sentences.daily: I would like to launch my own blog.
sentences.office: She would like to launch the new initiative.
sentences.interview: I would like to help launch new products.
sentences.business: We would like to launch in Q3.
sentences.formal: I would like to formally launch this program.
sentences.informal: When are you going to launch it?

words.usageNote: 'Launch a product/campaign/website'. 'Launch into' = suddenly start speaking/doing.

words.simpleMeaning: a worry or matter of interest; to relate to something

sentences.daily: I would like to address a concern.
sentences.office: She would like to raise a concern.
sentences.interview: I would like to express my concerns.
sentences.business: We would like to discuss a concern regarding the project.
sentences.formal: I would like to bring a concern to your attention.
sentences.informal: Can I share a concern with you?

words.usageNote: 'Raise/express/address a concern'. 'Concerning' = about (preposition): 'concerning this issue'.

words.simpleMeaning: the result or effect of something

sentences.daily: I would like a positive outcome.
sentences.office: She would like a successful outcome.
sentences.interview: I would like to contribute to good outcomes.
sentences.business: We would like to ensure a positive outcome.
sentences.formal: I would like to measure the expected outcome.
sentences.informal: What do you think the outcome will be?

words.usageNote: 'Positive/negative/desired outcome'. Common in project management and business.

words.simpleMeaning: the ability to assess and act without being told; a new plan

sentences.daily: I would like to take more initiative.
sentences.office: She would like to lead a new initiative.
sentences.interview: I would like to show initiative in this role.
sentences.business: We would like to launch a green initiative.
sentences.formal: I would like to introduce a cost-saving initiative.
sentences.informal: He takes a lot of initiative.

words.usageNote: 'Take/show/lead an initiative'. Very valued in workplace contexts.

words.simpleMeaning: to meet the needs or expectations of someone

sentences.daily: I would like to satisfy my family's expectations.
sentences.office: She would like to satisfy the client's requirements.
sentences.interview: I would like to satisfy all the job requirements.
sentences.business: We would like to satisfy every customer.
sentences.formal: I would like to satisfy the conditions of the contract.
sentences.informal: Would this satisfy you?

words.usageNote: 'Satisfy a need/requirement/customer'. 'Satisfied with' = happy about.

words.simpleMeaning: to make certain that something will happen

sentences.daily: I would like to ensure everyone is comfortable.
sentences.office: She would like to ensure quality standards.
sentences.interview: I would like to ensure project delivery on time.
sentences.business: We would like to ensure customer satisfaction.
sentences.formal: I would like to ensure full compliance.
sentences.informal: Can you ensure it's done by evening?

words.usageNote: 'Ensure that + clause' OR 'ensure something'. Stronger than 'make sure'.

words.simpleMeaning: able to wait without becoming frustrated

sentences.daily: I would like to be more patient.
sentences.office: She is always patient with new employees.
sentences.interview: I would like to handle pressure patiently.
sentences.business: We would like our team to remain patient.
sentences.formal: I would like to request that you be patient.
sentences.informal: Just be patient!

words.usageNote: Adjective: 'patient' /ˈpeɪʃənt/. Noun (person under medical care): same spelling, same pronunciation.

words.simpleMeaning: feeling at ease and relaxed

sentences.daily: I would like to feel comfortable in my new home.
sentences.office: She would like everyone to feel comfortable.
sentences.interview: I would like to make you feel comfortable.
sentences.business: We would like to ensure a comfortable experience.
sentences.formal: I would like to create a comfortable environment.
sentences.informal: Are you comfortable?

words.usageNote: 'Feel/make comfortable'. 'Comfortable with something' = at ease with it.

words.simpleMeaning: to say or do something in reply

sentences.daily: I would like to respond to your message today.
sentences.office: She responds to emails within an hour.
sentences.interview: I would like to respond to that with an example.
sentences.business: We would like to respond to the complaint.
sentences.formal: I would like to formally respond to your inquiry.
sentences.informal: Can you respond soon?

words.usageNote: 'Respond to someone/something'. More formal than 'reply'.

words.simpleMeaning: the final result of a process

sentences.daily: I hope the outcome is positive.
sentences.office: We need to track the outcome of this meeting.
sentences.interview: I would like to discuss the outcomes of my previous role.
sentences.business: The business outcome depends on planning.
sentences.formal: I would like to report on the expected outcomes.
sentences.informal: What's the outcome?

words.usageNote: See id:90. Reinforced here as it's central to business English.

words.simpleMeaning: to manage or deal with something

sentences.daily: I would like to handle this situation myself.
sentences.office: She handles difficult clients very well.
sentences.interview: I would like to handle challenging projects.
sentences.business: We would like to handle this account.
sentences.formal: I would like to handle this matter personally.
sentences.informal: Can you handle this?

words.usageNote: 'Handle a situation/problem/client'. Implies competence and control.

words.simpleMeaning: to link or get in touch with someone

sentences.daily: I would like to connect with like-minded people.
sentences.office: She would like to connect the two departments.
sentences.interview: I would like to connect with your team.
sentences.business: We would like to connect with global clients.
sentences.formal: I would like to connect with you professionally.
sentences.informal: Let's connect!

words.usageNote: 'Connect with someone' = make contact. Common on LinkedIn.

words.simpleMeaning: in a polite and respectful way

sentences.daily: Please speak respectfully to everyone.
sentences.office: She always communicates respectfully.
sentences.interview: I would like to respectfully disagree.
sentences.business: We would like to respectfully decline.
sentences.formal: I would like to respectfully submit this application.
sentences.informal: Can you say it more respectfully?

words.usageNote: 'Respectfully disagree/decline/submit' — classic formal phrases.

words.simpleMeaning: to work together toward a shared goal

sentences.daily: I would like to cooperate with my neighbors.
sentences.office: She would like to cooperate with other teams.
sentences.interview: I would like to cooperate fully with company policies.
sentences.business: We would like to cooperate on this initiative.
sentences.formal: I would like to request your cooperation.
sentences.informal: Please cooperate!

words.usageNote: 'Cooperate with someone'. 'Cooperation' = noun. British: 'co-operate'.

words.simpleMeaning: to recognize or admit the existence of something

sentences.daily: I would like to acknowledge your hard work.
sentences.office: She would like to acknowledge the team's success.
sentences.interview: I would like to acknowledge my area of improvement.
sentences.business: We would like to acknowledge your contribution.
sentences.formal: I would like to formally acknowledge this achievement.
sentences.informal: Can you acknowledge my message?

words.usageNote: 'Acknowledge receipt of' = confirm you received something. Common in emails.

words.simpleMeaning: well-prepared and refined

sentences.daily: I would like to give a polished performance.
sentences.office: Her reports are always polished.
sentences.interview: I would like to come across as polished and confident.
sentences.business: We would like to deliver a polished product.
sentences.formal: I would like to submit a polished draft.
sentences.informal: You look so polished!

words.usageNote: See id:85. 'Polished' in context of language/presentation = professional and smooth.

words.simpleMeaning: very keen or enthusiastic to do or have something

sentences.daily: I am eager to start this new journey.
sentences.office: She is eager to learn new skills.
sentences.interview: I am eager to contribute to your company.
sentences.business: We are eager to explore this partnership.
sentences.formal: I would like to express that I am eager.
sentences.informal: I'm so eager to try this!

words.usageNote: 'Eager to + infinitive'. 'Eagerness' = noun. Key word for showing enthusiasm in interviews.

words.simpleMeaning: the power or opportunity to act before others do

sentences.daily: I would like to show more initiative.
sentences.office: Taking initiative is appreciated here.
sentences.interview: I would like to demonstrate initiative in this role.
sentences.business: Initiative drives business growth.
sentences.formal: I would like to take the initiative on this project.
sentences.informal: Just take initiative!

words.usageNote: 'Take/show/use initiative'. 'On one's own initiative' = without being asked.
